title: Top 10 Must-Visit Attractions in Nakhon Phanom
meta_description: Discover the best attractions in Nakhon Phanom, Thailand — from ancient temples to stunning Mekong River experiences. Real prices, insider tips & local favorites.
intro: I'll never forget my first morning in Nakhon Phanom, watching the mist rise from the Mekong River while golden temple spires caught the dawn light. This northeastern Thai province, sitting directly across from Laos, feels like Thailand's best-kept secret. Unlike the crowded temples of Bangkok, Nakhon Phanom offers an intimate glimpse into authentic Isan culture, where Vietnamese heritage meets Thai tradition. The province's strategic location along the Mekong has shaped centuries of history, visible in colonial-era architecture and the spiritual depth of its monasteries. During my week here, I discovered that the real magic isn't found in crowds—it's in the quiet moments watching fishermen work at dawn, the reverent silence inside centuries-old temples, and conversations with locals who remember when the border was different. The best time to visit is November to February, when temperatures cool to 25-28°C and the river flows at its clearest. Whether you're seeking spiritual enlightenment, historical understanding, or simply a slower pace of travel, Nakhon Phanom rewards those who venture beyond Thailand's usual tourist circuits with experiences that linger long after you leave.

items.name: Ho Chi Minh House Museum
items.story: Walking into this modest timber house, I felt transported to 1924. Ho Chi Minh lived here for eight months while working incognito at a local print shop, during his revolutionary years. The narrow wooden structure, perched above street level in traditional Indochinese style, contains sparse but meaningful exhibits—photographs, documents, and personal artifacts that reveal his life before becoming Vietnam's leader. The single bedroom, simple desk, and communal areas feel almost frozen in time. A knowledgeable caretaker shared stories about how locals never knew his true identity until decades later. The museum's courtyard offers views of the Mekong, the same river Ho Chi Minh gazed upon over a century ago. Walking through each room, I was struck by how a future revolutionary lived anonymously among ordinary people, grinding political philosophy while earning wages as a laborer. The museum captures an intimate, lesser-known chapter of Vietnamese history that most travelers miss entirely.
items.why_locals_love_it: Locals take pride in this historical connection, viewing the museum as proof that Nakhon Phanom played a role in Southeast Asian independence movements. It represents shared Lao-Vietnamese-Thai cultural heritage and attracts history scholars from across the region.
insider_tips: Visit early morning (8-9am) to avoid heat and enjoy peaceful exploration
Hire a local guide at the museum entrance (150-200 THB) for detailed historical context and anecdotes
Photography is allowed; best light for photos is between 8-10am on the east-facing facade
Combine with a Mekong riverside walk—the museum is near the promenade
items.price_reality: Entrance fee: 50 THB (~$1.40 USD). Incredibly affordable for a museum of this historical significance. No discounts for groups, but entry is free for Vietnamese nationals with passport.
items.location_details: Located at 183 Thanon Sunthon Wijit, near the Mekong Promenade in central Nakhon Phanom. Accessible by songthaew from the bus station (15-20 THB). Walking distance from riverside hotels.
items.personal_moment: An elderly Vietnamese visitor quietly stood before a photograph for several minutes, tears in her eyes. Without exchanging words, we both understood we were witnessing something sacred—a bridge between nations, built by one man's hidden determination decades ago.

items.name: Mekong Promenade
items.story: The Mekong Promenade stretches for kilometers along the river, and I spent hours simply sitting on weathered benches, watching the boundary between two nations flow before me. The wide, paved walkway is lined with manicured gardens, occasional food stalls, and locals going about their evening routines. By sunset, the entire promenade transforms—joggers, families, monks collecting alms, and travelers all converge in a peaceful parade. I watched fishermen casting nets in traditional techniques unchanged for generations, their movements fluid and meditative. The river itself is hypnotic—broad, powerful, and impossibly peaceful. On the Laotian side, you can see mirror temples and villages that seem suspended in another era. The promenade's charm lies in its unpretentiousness; there are no entrance gates, no crowds pushing for photos. Instead, there's genuine community life: an elderly couple holding hands watching the sunset, children playing despite the heat, vendors selling papaya salad to evening walkers. The Promenade connects Nakhon Phanom to its lifeblood—the Mekong—and standing here, you understand why this region has always been defined by this river.
items.why_locals_love_it: Locals use the promenade daily for exercise, meditation, and social connection. It's free, accessible, and represents the heart of Nakhon Phanom's identity as a Mekong city. Evening walks here are a cherished ritual.
insider_tips: Visit at sunset (around 5:45-6:30pm) for the most atmospheric experience and cooler temperatures
Bring cash for small food vendors—pad thai, grilled fish, fresh fruit—all 40-80 THB
The northern section (near Wat Nong Saeng) is less crowded than the central promenade
Go early morning (5:30-7am) to see monks collecting alms and traditional fishing practices
items.price_reality: Completely free. No entrance fee, no hidden charges. Food and drinks from vendors are inexpensive (30-100 THB per item).
items.location_details: Runs along the Mekong riverfront in central Nakhon Phanom. Main access points near Thanon Sunthon Wijit and near major hotels. Connected by walking paths; songthaew stops at multiple points.
items.personal_moment: A young child offered me a flower she'd picked, shy but determined. I accepted with gratitude, and her grandmother laughed warmly—a moment of human connection that transcended language, the promenade's most precious gift.

items.name: Lao-Style Golden Stupas
items.story: The Buddhist temples of Nakhon Phanom showcase a fascinating architectural blend—Thai designs merged with distinctly Lao influences visible in their golden stupas and unique spire designs. Wat Phra That Narai Cheng Weng, the most significant temple, features a breathtaking 35-meter stupa covered in gold leaf that glitters impossibly bright in morning light. Walking barefoot through the temple grounds in the pre-dawn quiet, I encountered monks chanting in Pali, their voices echoing off ancient stone. The stupa's base contains intricate carvings depicting Buddhist stories, and devotees circled it clockwise in silent prayer. The Lao influence is unmistakable—the stupa's proportions, the way it tapers toward heaven, the surrounding smaller stupas, even the temple's spatial arrangement all reflect centuries of cross-border cultural exchange. I watched elderly visitors leave offerings of marigolds and incense, their actions deliberate and reverent. Unlike tourist-heavy temples elsewhere, here I was surrounded by genuine pilgrims seeking spiritual connection, not photo opportunities. The temple hosts important festivals during Buddhist holidays, when thousands gather. Standing before the golden stupa at dawn, I understood how sacred architecture can transcend politics and borders, speaking directly to the human need for transcendence.
items.why_locals_love_it: These temples are spiritual anchors for the community, representing shared Lao-Thai Buddhist heritage. They're pilgrimage sites for devotion, not just tourist attractions, lending authentic spiritual atmosphere tourists rarely experience.
insider_tips: Dress conservatively: cover shoulders and knees. Women should wear loose pants or long skirts; men should wear shirts
Remove shoes before entering main prayer halls; use provided shoe racks
Visit between 5-7am for morning chanting sessions and the most serene atmosphere
Make small donations (20-50 THB) to support temple maintenance; incense sticks are usually free
items.price_reality: Entrance fee: Free. Donations are entirely voluntary and suggested amounts are 20-100 THB to support temple operations. Photography inside prayer halls may be restricted—always ask permission first.
items.location_details: Wat Phra That Narai Cheng Weng is located in Talad Noi district, about 8km south of city center. Accessible by songthaew (20-30 THB) or motorcycle taxi (100-150 THB). Several other golden stupas are within 5km radius.
items.personal_moment: A monk noticed my obvious reverence and, despite our language barrier, spent ten minutes gesturing explanations of the stupa's symbolic elements. His patience and generosity in sharing his spiritual home moved me profoundly.

items.name: Sunset Mekong River Cruise
items.story: I booked a sunset cruise expecting tourists and disappointment, but found instead a meditative journey into the river's soul. The small wooden boat, decorated with simple flower garlands, pushed away from the dock as afternoon light mellowed into gold. We glided past fishing villages where life continues as it has for centuries—wooden houses on stilts, nets drying on bamboo racks, children waving from riverbanks. The cruise captain, a weathered man named Somchai, explained how the Mekong sustains everything here: fish for meals, water for crops, the spiritual boundary between nations. As we drifted, he pointed out Laotian temples across the water, their golden spires mirroring ours. When sunset arrived, the entire sky ignited—orange, pink, crimson spreading across both sky and water until the river itself seemed to burn. Other boats disappeared; we were alone with the moment. A group of fishermen's boats passed, their occupants waving. The light faded slowly, reluctantly, as if the river didn't want to release the day. By the time we returned to dock, darkness had completely claimed the landscape, stars emerging above the water in impossible clarity. That evening, I understood why ancient cultures deified rivers—this one demands reverence.
items.why_locals_love_it: Locals view sunset cruises as a way to reconnect with the Mekong that defines their identity. The experience honors their river heritage and provides livelihood for boat operators and their families.
insider_tips: Book cruises directly through your hotel or Nakhon Phanom tourism office (200-400 THB for budget cruises)
Schedule for 1.5-2 hours starting 90 minutes before sunset for optimal light
Bring light jacket—river breeze cools quickly as sun sets, even in warm months
Ask captain about fishing techniques; many offer explanations of traditional Mekong livelihoods
items.price_reality: Typical sunset cruise: 250-400 THB (~$7-11 USD) per person for 1.5-hour journey. Premium cruises with dinner available 500-800 THB. Advance booking recommended during peak season.
items.location_details: Departs from boat docks along the Mekong Promenade. Main departure point near city center hotels. Tours arranged through hotel concierge or directly at dock ticket offices.
items.personal_moment: As sunset peaked, a young girl on our boat gasped—it was her first time seeing this. Watching wonder reflected in her eyes reminded me that some moments never lose their magic, no matter how many times witnessed.

items.name: Traditional Mekong Fishermen Scene
items.story: Before dawn, I met a fisherman named Chai at a riverside dock, arranging to join him for a morning catch. We launched his simple wooden boat into mist-covered water, so thick I could barely see the opposite bank. Chai's movements were economical and knowing—he'd worked this river for forty years. He demonstrated the circular throwing technique for casting nets, a motion that looked simple until I tried and nearly fell overboard. The net unfurled like a fabric flower, hitting water with a satisfying splash, then Chai waited with meditative patience. When he pulled it up, silver fish flashed in the emerging light. This wasn't performance; it was genuine subsistence fishing. We spoke little—Chai's English was minimal—but our shared focus created understanding. He explained in gestures how the river's seasons determine fishing success, how dams upstream affect fish patterns, how his grandchildren increasingly choose city work over river life. By the time morning light fully arrived, we'd caught enough for his family's meals and modest sale. Returning to dock, Chai invited me for coffee, where his wife sold small portions of fish soup to passing workers. This wasn't a tourist experience; it was a rare glimpse into livelihoods rarely documented. The Mekong sustains these people through skill, knowledge, and direct relationship with nature—something industrialized societies have largely abandoned.
items.why_locals_love_it: These fishing traditions represent cultural continuity and pride in self-sufficient, nature-connected livelihoods. Locals value fishermen as keepers of ancient knowledge and sustainable practices.
insider_tips: Hire fishermen directly through hotels or tourism offices; costs 400-600 THB for 2-3 hour morning session
Go very early (4:30-5:00am start) to experience fishing before heat peaks
Wear water-resistant clothing and shoes with grip; boats are slippery
Bring cash for tea/coffee at riverside stalls; negotiate prices beforehand (usually 20-30 THB)
items.price_reality: Private fishing experience: 400-600 THB (~$11-17 USD) per person for 2-3 hours. Group rates available. Breakfast at fishermen's family stalls: 40-60 THB.
items.location_details: Fishermen congregate at various docks along the Mekong, especially near Talad Noi and northern promenade areas. Hotels can arrange meetings with specific fishermen experienced with tourists.
items.personal_moment: Chai's grandson appeared at dawn, watching his grandfather with pride. When Chai presented him the best fish caught, the boy's face shone with possibility—a future generation inheriting river wisdom.

items.name: Sacred Naga Spirit Lake
items.story: The drive to Naga Spirit Lake (approximately 40km northwest, near Bueng Kan province border) reveals landscape that grows progressively wilder. The lake itself is sacred to local communities—believed to be home to the mystical Naga, the serpent spirit of Buddhist and Hindu mythology. Legend holds that a Naga protects the lake and surrounding lands, and locals maintain deep reverence for this belief. The lake's setting is dramatic: dark water hemmed by forested hills creating an almost primordial atmosphere. I visited with a local guide, Niran, who explained the lake's spiritual significance and the annual merit-making ceremonies held here. Unlike commercialized sacred sites, this location remains genuinely sacred—tourist infrastructure is minimal. A few simple spirit houses stand at the water's edge, adorned with offerings of flowers and incense. Niran told me stories of reported Naga sightings, which locals take seriously. Whether you believe in the Naga or not, the lake possesses undeniable power—an energy that comes from centuries of spiritual attention and natural beauty untouched by development. We walked the perimeter as afternoon light slanted through forest canopy, encountering only monks on alms rounds and a few local devotees. The lake demands respect rather than entertaining; you leave feeling you've witnessed something sacred, something that exists outside tourist frameworks.
items.why_locals_love_it: The lake represents connection to pre-Buddhist animism and spiritual traditions predating organized religion. Locals honor it as a place where the natural and supernatural touch, deserving protection and reverence.
insider_tips: Visit with a local guide (arrange through Nakhon Phanom tourism office, 600-800 THB for guide)
Wear modest clothing—this is a sacred site; locals dress respectfully
Bring offerings if spiritually inclined (flowers, incense available at local shops, 30-50 THB)
Go early morning or late afternoon; mid-day heat is intense and midday feels less spiritually resonant
items.price_reality: No entrance fee. Local guide: 600-800 THB for half-day. Transportation: hire motorcycle taxi (400-600 THB round trip) or arrange through hotel. Offerings: 30-100 THB.
items.location_details: Located in Bueng Kan Province, approximately 40km from Nakhon Phanom city. Accessible by motorcycle or private vehicle. No public transportation directly to the lake; guide arrangements essential.
items.personal_moment: Niran placed flowers at the spirit house and closed his eyes in quiet prayer. I realized tourism here wasn't about entertainment but about being welcomed into genuine spiritual practice—a privilege requiring humility and respect.

items.name: Wat Phra That Panom
items.story: Towering above the city center, Wat Phra That Panom's 57-meter ancient stupa serves as Nakhon Phanom's spiritual centerpiece and most recognizable landmark. The temple's origins date to the 10th century, making it one of the region's oldest structures. Climbing the 200+ steps to the stupa base, I felt the effort—Nakhon Phanom's subtropical heat doesn't forgive exertion—but the panoramic reward justified every breath. From the top, the entire city unfolds: the sinuous Mekong, Laotian landscape beyond, residential areas, and distant forests. The stupa itself, rebuilt after various reconstructions, maintains classical proportions despite modern repairs. Prayer bells hang from the structure; I added my small rings to the cacophony of thousands before. Inside the main hall, Buddha images receive constant devotion—locals making merit with flowers, incense, and prostrations. The temple complex includes multiple buildings, meditation areas, and peaceful gardens where time seems negotiable. Unlike crowded Bangkok temples, Wat Phra That Panom hosts genuine pilgrims alongside tourists, creating layered spiritual atmosphere. Walking the grounds, I observed how the temple has adapted to modernity while maintaining spiritual integrity—new buildings stand beside ancient stupas without jarring contradiction. The evening light touching the main stupa's gold surface felt transcendent, as though centuries of prayers had accumulated into visible luminescence.
items.why_locals_love_it: As the city's spiritual anchor and oldest sacred site, Wat Phra That Panom unifies community identity. Major festivals here draw thousands; locals view the temple as protecting Nakhon Phanom's spiritual welfare.
insider_tips: Wear modest dress: covered shoulders and knees required for prayer halls
Climbing to stupa base requires fitness; go early morning before heat peaks
Entrance fee includes access to museum—allocate 2-3 hours for thorough exploration
English-speaking monks sometimes available for temple history explanations; ask at main office
items.price_reality: Entrance fee: 20 THB (~$0.55 USD). Donation suggestions for merit-making: 100-500 THB. Museum access included in entrance fee.
items.location_details: Located at Thanon Sunthon Wijit in central Nakhon Phanom, near the Mekong Promenade. Walking distance from hotels; songthaew stops nearby (10-15 THB from city center).
items.personal_moment: An elderly woman rang a prayer bell three times with focused intention, then sat in extended meditation. Her presence reminded me that sacred spaces work differently for those who approach them with genuine spiritual need rather than touristic curiosity.

items.name: Nakhon Phanom Walking Street Market
items.story: Saturday evenings transform Thanon Sunthon Wijit into Walking Street Market—a vibrant cascade of food stalls, local crafts, and humanity that embodies Nakhon Phanom's community spirit. I arrived at dusk as vendors arranged their spaces: grilled fish on stick-skewers, sticky rice in bamboo containers, fresh spring rolls, mango salads, and mysterious fried treats I couldn't identify. The market stretches for several blocks, completely pedestrianized, crowded but never chaotic. I followed my nose and stomach, stopping at a grilled fish stall where the vendor, noticing my obvious tourist status, filled my plate generously and charged me 40 THB—a price that seemed impossible. Nearby, a woman made papaya salad to order, pounding it in a mortar with precise movements honed through decades. At a clothing stall, I watched friends shopping and laughing, touching fabrics they'd seen hundreds of times. This wasn't tourist theater; it was authentic community gathering where locals shop, socialize, and sustain small business relationships. I sat on plastic stools with strangers who became conversationalists, sharing food and gestures that transcended language. The market pulses with energy, color, and sensory abundance—the real Thailand that guidebooks struggle to capture. By 10pm, the market wind down, vendors packing methodically, the street returning to ordinary daytime commerce. That evening, I ate the best food of my week for under 200 THB.
items.why_locals_love_it: Walking Street Market is primary social hub and shopping destination for locals. It supports small vendors, strengthens community bonds, and represents Nakhon Phanom's self-sufficiency and commercial pride.
insider_tips: Go Saturday evenings 5-10pm; that's the official Walking Street time with maximum vendors
Bring cash; most small vendors don't accept cards
Arrive 6-7pm for freshest food and best vendor selection; by 9pm selection thins
Ask vendors names and try to remember them; personal connection increases friendliness and can lead to better prices
items.price_reality: Food stalls: 30-80 THB per item. Clothing/crafts: 100-500 THB. Easily feed yourself for 150-250 THB. Expect no price inflation for tourists.
items.location_details: Thanon Sunthon Wijit in central Nakhon Phanom, extending for several blocks. Walking distance from most central hotels. Saturday evenings only.
items.personal_moment: A vendor's young daughter practiced English phrases with me while her mother prepared food. The girl's determination to communicate created a bridge between our worlds that neither of us would forget.

items.name: Wat Nong Saeng
items.story: This smaller temple, located along the northern Mekong Promenade, offers intimate Buddhist experience without crowds that characterize Nakhon Phanom's larger temples. The main hall features intricately carved wooden doors depicting Buddhist narratives, their craftsmanship remarkable in detail. Inside, a peaceful Buddha image receives offerings from locals; incense smoke patterns create patterns against natural light filtered through windows. The temple's garden includes vegetable plots and simple dwellings for resident monks, demonstrating how monasteries function as self-sustaining communities. I sat in the garden's shade, watching monks moving between structures with purposeful tranquility. Unlike tourism-oriented temples, Wat Nong Saeng maintains quiet focus on spiritual practice. The abbot, noticing my respectful observation, invited me for tea and conversed in slow, clear English about Buddhist philosophy and monastic life. He explained how meditation shapes perspective, how material simplicity clarifies what matters. Sitting in the simple wooden temple, sipping tea while listening to his wisdom, I experienced Buddhism not as exotic mysticism but as practical spiritual technology for living well. The temple's modest appearance belies deep spiritual significance—it's exactly the kind of place most tourists miss, which is precisely why it matters. Leaving, I understood that sacred spaces don't require architectural grandeur; intention and genuine practice create holiness.
items.why_locals_love_it: Wat Nong Saeng serves local community spiritually and practically—monks perform ceremonies, provide counseling, and maintain cultural traditions without tourist infrastructure distracting from spiritual mission.
insider_tips: Dress modestly; this is working temple, not tourist attraction
Visit during monastic meal times (7-8am or 11am-noon) when spiritual activity is most visible
Monks appreciate sincere questions about Buddhism more than casual visits
Donations are entirely voluntary; no suggested amounts posted
items.price_reality: Completely free. Donations entirely voluntary (20-100 THB suggested, not required). Tea/simple refreshments sometimes offered at no cost.
items.location_details: Located on northern section of Mekong Promenade, approximately 2km north of city center. Accessible by songthaew (10-15 THB) or walking from promenade.
items.personal_moment: The abbot's gentle eyes reflected decades of meditation practice. When he spoke about compassion's transformative power, his words carried authority that came only from lived experience—I felt Buddhist philosophy as truth, not theory.

items.name: Nakhon Phanom Museum
items.story: The Nakhon Phanom Museum, housed in a colonial-era building that itself constitutes an artifact, chronicles the region's complex history through photographs, artifacts, and documents spanning centuries. The French colonial period is well-represented—architectural designs, administrative records, and personal effects of European residents reveal how colonialism shaped the region's development. Exhibits trace Vietnamese immigration, Lao cultural influences, and the region's role in various Thai kingdoms. The museum's strongest section documents the American War period, addressing how proximity to Laos affected local communities. I spent hours examining photographs of daily life across decades—street scenes, festivals, family portraits—realizing how photography preserves human dignity even in difficult historical moments. An elderly guard, seeing my engagement, shared personal stories connecting exhibits to his own family's experiences. The museum makes clear that Nakhon Phanom isn't a singular culture but a convergence zone where multiple traditions intersected, sometimes harmoniously, sometimes conflictually. The basement contains Buddhist artifacts and prehistoric findings, extending history beyond written records. While not architecturally grand, the museum succeeds through authenticity—it's curated by locals who lived this history, not distant scholars. Exiting, I possessed new understanding of Nakhon Phanom's identity as shaped by geography, empires, and human movements across borders.
items.why_locals_love_it: The museum preserves community memory and validates local history as significant to larger regional narratives. It's a point of cultural pride, documenting ancestors' experiences with respectful attention.
insider_tips: Allow 1.5-2 hours for thorough exploration; displays reward careful attention
Guide services available (inquire at entrance); conversations with guides often exceed formal exhibits
Photography generally allowed except in specific religious artifact sections
Visit early morning for coolest temperatures and better museum lighting
items.price_reality: Entrance fee: 30 THB (~$0.85 USD). Guide services: 100-150 THB additional. Excellent value for historical depth provided.
items.location_details: Located in colonial-era building in central Nakhon Phanom. Accessible by walking from city center or short songthaew ride (10-15 THB). Near government buildings.
items.personal_moment: The guard showed me his grandfather's photograph in a museum display—a man he'd known, now preserved as historical record. The convergence of personal history with public documentation moved us both into reflective silence.

local_wisdom: Nakhon Phanom rewards patience and slow travel. Rather than rushing through attractions, spend extended time in single locations—sit with monks, share meals with vendors, sit by the Mekong. The province's magic emerges through accumulated moments, not checked boxes. Learn basic Thai greetings; locals profoundly appreciate effort to speak their language. Respect sacred spaces with genuine reverence, not performative respect. Visit temples during monk activities rather than empty hours—their presence animates spiritual atmosphere. The best experiences happen through unplanned conversations and willingness to say yes to local invitations. Nakhon Phanom moves slower than Thailand's tourist centers; embrace this rhythm rather than fighting it.
budget_hacks: Food stalls at Walking Street Market and riverside vendors offer incredible value (30-80 THB per meal) compared to tourist restaurants (200-400 THB). Eat where locals eat and save 70% while eating better.
Many attractions are completely free (Mekong Promenade, temples, portions of markets). Strategic planning of free activities between paid experiences keeps budgets minimal.
Songthaew transportation costs 10-30 THB for any city journey; motorcycle taxis 50-100 THB. Never use tourist taxis charging 5-10x standard rates. Ask hotel staff for standard prices before journeys.
Guesthouses in Nakhon Phanom cost 200-400 THB/night for quality rooms, far cheaper than Bangkok. Longer stays often negotiate even lower rates. Allocate 400-600 THB/day for comfortable budget travel.
cultural_notes: Buddhist temple etiquette is crucial: never point feet toward Buddha images, remove shoes before prayer halls, speak softly, avoid standing above monks or passing over their heads. Dress conservatively (shoulders and knees covered) at all temples and sacred sites.

The Mekong River carries spiritual significance in local cosmology; treat it with respect. Never dispose of trash in the river; participate in locals' reverence for this life-giving waterway that defines the region's identity.

Laos and Vietnam are extremely recent histories for Nakhon Phanom residents—avoid insensitive comments about neighboring countries or recent conflicts. The region's complex border history remains emotionally present for older generations.

Respect for elders and monks is paramount in Thai culture. Always greet with wai gesture (hands pressed together at chest level), yield seating to elderly and monks on transportation, and address people with appropriate titles (khun for adults, phra for monks).
